issues.details: Add an expedited call (e.g., 3-5 business days) for identified transactions, paired with the bridge facility and GP-advance mechanics as a backstop.

issues.category: D. Capital calls, speed, and warehousing
issues.title: Can the GP call capital for deposits, broken-deal expenses, legal diligence, and reserves
issues.prompt: Can the GP call capital for deposits, broken-deal expenses, legal diligence, and reserves?
issues.details: Make sure the call mechanics and the Fund Expenses definition both cover deposits and broken-deal costs, which are routine in competitive secondary processes.

issues.category: D. Capital calls, speed, and warehousing
issues.title: What default remedies apply if an LP misses a capital call
issues.prompt: What default remedies apply if an LP misses a capital call?
issues.details: The form leaves remedies to the fund agreement. Standard menu: interest on late amounts, dilution/forfeiture up to 50%, forced transfer, and loss of voting rights.

issues.category: D. Capital calls, speed, and warehousing
issues.title: Can the fund borrow under a bridge facility or subscription line
issues.prompt: Can the fund borrow under a bridge facility or subscription line?
issues.details: The form permits borrowing subject to a bracketed cap tied to commitments or uncalled commitments. Pick the cap (commonly 20-30% of commitments) and decide whether guarantees and leverage at SPV or holding-vehicle level count against it - with deals executed through SPVs, a fund-level-only cap is meaningless. Fund borrowing can also create UBTI for tax-exempt LPs.

issues.category: D. Capital calls, speed, and warehousing
issues.title: Can the GP or affiliate advance funds to the fund
issues.prompt: Can the GP or affiliate advance funds to the fund?
issues.details: GP advances are the fastest bridge for a closing deadline. Authorize them expressly, with interest at cost and repayment from the next call to avoid a conflicts fight later.

issues.category: D. Capital calls, speed, and warehousing
issues.title: What approvals are needed for warehoused investments
issues.prompt: What approvals are needed for warehoused investments?
issues.details: Affiliate sales to the fund are classic conflict territory: decide whether cost-plus-expenses transfers within a set window are pre-authorized or need LPAC sign-off.

issues.category: D. Capital calls, speed, and warehousing
issues.title: How are warehousing costs, interest, legal fees, and conflict disclosures handled
issues.prompt: How are warehousing costs, interest, legal fees, and conflict disclosures handled?
issues.details: Define the transfer price formula (cost plus carrying costs at a stated rate) and disclose the arrangement in the PPM conflicts section.

issues.title: Who can solicit LPs
issues.prompt: Who can solicit LPs?
issues.details: Principals and Manager personnel can solicit under the issuer exemption; anyone compensated for raising capital raises broker-dealer issues. Inventory the expected placement parties before signing anything.

issues.title: Must any compensated LP placement be through a registered broker-dealer
issues.prompt: Must any compensated LP placement be through a registered broker-dealer?
issues.details: Transaction-based compensation to an unregistered finder is the classic foot-fault; the safe default is BD-only for success-fee arrangements.

issues.title: Can non-BD finders or consultants be used, and under what restrictions
issues.prompt: Can non-BD finders or consultants be used, and under what restrictions?
issues.details: Only with flat-fee/consulting structures that avoid transaction-based pay, no negotiation role, and counsel sign-off. Document the analysis per relationship.
issues.answer: yes ideally, and we would like to potentially give carry

issues.title: Can placement compensation include cash fees, management fee sharing, carried interest, revenue share, or expense reimbursement
issues.prompt: Can placement compensation include cash fees, management fee sharing, carried interest, revenue share, or expense reimbursement?
issues.details: Carry-sharing is usually the cleanest for non-BD parties because it is an internal GP/CarryCo split rather than a fund-paid fee, but it still needs conflicts disclosure and BD analysis if tied to commitments raised.

issues.title: Is compensation paid by the Fund, GP, Manager, CarryCo, or affiliate
issues.prompt: Is compensation paid by the Fund, GP, Manager, CarryCo, or affiliate?
issues.details: Who pays determines who bears the cost: Fund-paid placement fees are LP money (and often trigger a fee offset); Manager/GP-paid comes out of sponsor economics.

issues.title: Is the compensation treated as Fund Expense, Manager expense, or GP/carry sharing
issues.prompt: Is the compensation treated as Fund Expense, Manager expense, or GP/carry sharing?
issues.details: Keep the term sheet, LPA expense section, and offset provision consistent - LPs read all three, and mismatches invite negotiation.

issues.title: Will placement-linked carry require disclosure in the PPM and/or term sheet
issues.prompt: Will placement-linked carry require disclosure in the PPM and/or term sheet?
issues.details: Disclose the category of arrangement (carry sharing with placement parties) in the PPM conflicts/fees sections; specific splits can stay in the separate agreements.

issues.title: Will placement parties receive a tail on LPs introduced before termination
issues.prompt: Will placement parties receive a tail on LPs introduced before termination?
issues.details: A tail is a placement-agreement term, not a fund-document term; decide the standard tail (commonly 6-12 months) for the form agreement.

issues.title: Who approves offering materials and investor communications
issues.prompt: Who approves offering materials and investor communications?
issues.details: The Manager should have sole authority over offering materials, with placement parties contractually barred from creating their own.
issues.answer: Management LLC

issues.title: How will 506(b) relationship rules or 506(c) verification rules be handled
issues.prompt: How will 506(b) relationship rules or 506(c) verification rules be handled?
issues.details: 506(b) requires pre-existing relationships and no general solicitation; 506(c) allows solicitation but requires accreditation verification. Placement-party outreach practices must match whichever path is chosen.

issues.title: Who can source secondary opportunities
issues.prompt: Who can source secondary opportunities?
issues.details: Anyone can introduce deals; the design question is who may be compensated and how. Keep investment authority with the GP regardless of source.

issues.title: Can sourcing parties receive cash fees, deal-by-deal carry, fund carry, or revenue share
issues.prompt: Can sourcing parties receive cash fees, deal-by-deal carry, fund carry, or revenue share?
issues.details: Deal-by-deal carry to a source is a fund-economics event that needs waterfall or GP-split plumbing; cash success fees raise BD issues if tied to securities transactions.

issues.title: Can a deal source negotiate terms, or only introduce opportunities
issues.prompt: Can a deal source negotiate terms, or only introduce opportunities?
issues.details: Introduction-only keeps the source on the safer side of the broker-dealer line; negotiation authority plus success comp looks like brokerage.

issues.title: Can a deal source also represent sellers
issues.prompt: Can a deal source also represent sellers?
issues.details: Dual representation is a live conflict: require disclosure, and decide whether LPAC consent is needed when a compensated source is on both sides.

issues.title: Do deal-source payments create broker-dealer concerns
issues.prompt: Do deal-source payments create broker-dealer concerns?
issues.details: Yes if compensation is transaction-based and the source effects securities transactions. This is a counsel sign-off item for each sourcing agreement, not a one-time decision.

issues.title: Are sourcing fees Fund Expenses or Manager/GP expenses
issues.prompt: Are sourcing fees Fund Expenses or Manager/GP expenses?
issues.details: The form's GP Expenses section brackets whether investment-sourcing costs sit with the sponsor. Decide the default and mirror it in the sourcing form agreement.

issues.title: Are there exclusivity, non-circumvention, or tail periods
issues.prompt: Are there exclusivity, non-circumvention, or tail periods?
issues.details: These live in the sourcing agreement; set house-standard positions (no exclusivity, narrow non-circumvention, short tail) for the template.

issues.title: What disclosures are required for affiliated or related-party sourcing
issues.prompt: What disclosures are required for affiliated or related-party sourcing?
issues.details: Affiliate-sourced deals should be disclosed in the PPM conflicts section and may need LPAC consent under the investment limitations.

issues.title: How are conflicts handled if a source also participates in another vehicle
issues.prompt: How are conflicts handled if a source also participates in another vehicle?
issues.details: Combine allocation-policy treatment with LPAC disclosure: a source with its own SPV competing for the same allocation is the main real-world scenario.

issues.title: Who has authority to approve sourced deals
issues.prompt: Who has authority to approve sourced deals?
issues.details: The GP's investment committee approves all deals; sources never hold approval rights. Only conflicted deals escalate to the LPAC.

issues.category: G. Future funds, SPVs, and allocations
issues.title: What counts as a prohibited substantially similar blind-pool fund
issues.prompt: What counts as a prohibited substantially similar blind-pool fund?
issues.details: Define narrowly: a multi-investor blind pool with a substantially overlapping mandate. Single-deal SPVs, co-invests, and feeders should be expressly excluded.

issues.category: G. Future funds, SPVs, and allocations
issues.title: What carveouts are needed for co-invest vehicles, single-deal SPVs, feeders, blockers, alternative investment vehicles, holding vehicles, and overflow vehicles
issues.prompt: What carveouts are needed for co-invest vehicles, single-deal SPVs, feeders, blockers, alternative investment vehicles, holding vehicles, and overflow vehicles?
issues.details: List every execution-vehicle type as an express carveout from the successor-fund restriction; this is the memo's core drafting request and the biggest execution risk if missed.

issues.category: G. Future funds, SPVs, and allocations
issues.title: Can the Manager manage multiple funds at once
issues.prompt: Can the Manager manage multiple funds at once?
issues.details: The form already permits other competitive activity; make sure the Manager's other mandates only trip the restriction if they are substantially similar blind pools.

issues.category: G. Future funds, SPVs, and allocations
issues.title: How are opportunities allocated among Fund I, Fund II, co-invests, parallel funds, SPVs, affiliates, and insiders
issues.prompt: How are opportunities allocated among Fund I, Fund II, co-invests, parallel funds, SPVs, affiliates, and insiders?
issues.details: Adopt a written allocation policy (fund-first with capacity-based overflow to co-invest/SPVs is typical) and reference it in the PPM; regulators and LPs both ask for it.

issues.category: G. Future funds, SPVs, and allocations
issues.title: Can LPs get co-investment rights
issues.prompt: Can LPs get co-investment rights?
issues.details: The form keeps co-invest offers discretionary. Hold that line in the LPA and grant any priority rights through side letters so MFN exposure is controlled.

issues.category: G. Future funds, SPVs, and allocations
issues.title: Can insiders invest personally alongside the fund
issues.prompt: Can insiders invest personally alongside the fund?
issues.details: Permit only after the fund takes its full allocation, with conflicts disclosure; unconstrained insider co-investment is a common LP diligence objection.

issues.category: G. Future funds, SPVs, and allocations
issues.title: Can the fund invest through a vehicle that charges its own fees/carry
issues.prompt: Can the fund invest through a vehicle that charges its own fees/carry?
issues.details: Secondary access often requires buying into fee-bearing SPVs. Permit it with a cap or LPAC consent, and disclose the fee layering; a flat prohibition would block core strategy deals.

issues.category: G. Future funds, SPVs, and allocations
issues.title: Will LPAC approval be needed for certain conflicts or allocation decisions
issues.prompt: Will LPAC approval be needed for certain conflicts or allocation decisions?
issues.details: Reserve LPAC approval for genuine conflicts (affiliate transactions, warehousing outside pre-agreed terms, allocation exceptions) and keep routine execution GP-discretionary. Settle the LPAC's composition at the same time: the form brackets member count and appointment mechanics, and since the LPAC will carry real weight here (whitelist changes, warehousing, conflicts), keep it small (3-5 GP-appointed members from the largest LPs) and define its approval list precisely.

issues.title: Will the manager rely on an investment adviser exemption or register
issues.prompt: Will the manager rely on an investment adviser exemption or register?
issues.details: Most sub-$150M private fund managers use the SEC private fund adviser exemption (ERA status) plus state rules. The answer drives compliance obligations across the platform.

issues.title: What state-level adviser requirements apply
issues.prompt: What state-level adviser requirements apply?
issues.details: Check the manager's home state: some states require investment adviser registration even for exempt-reporting advisers.

issues.title: Who handles AML/KYC and bad actor checks
issues.prompt: Who handles AML/KYC and bad actor checks?
issues.details: Usually the fund administrator runs AML/KYC on subscriptions; Rule 506(d) bad-actor checks must also cover placement parties and beneficial owners of the GP.

issues.title: Who is fund administrator
issues.prompt: Who is fund administrator?
issues.details: Select early - the administrator drives capital-call logistics, reporting cadence, and AML/KYC workflow, and admin fees are a named Fund Expense.

issues.title: Who values private positions and SPV interests
issues.prompt: Who values private positions and SPV interests?
issues.details: The GP values assets in good faith under the form, but stale secondary marks are a known regulator focus; adopt a written valuation policy covering indirect SPV positions and in-kind distributions.

issues.title: What cyber/privacy/data-room procedures are needed
issues.prompt: What cyber/privacy/data-room procedures are needed?
issues.details: Operational item: data-room access controls and investor-data handling belong in the compliance manual, not the fund documents.

issues.title: What insurance and indemnity coverage should be maintained
issues.prompt: What insurance and indemnity coverage should be maintained?
issues.details: GP/Manager D&O and E&O coverage backstops the fund indemnity; the form lets the fund bear the premium as a Fund Expense. Confirm the indemnity's conduct standard too - the form exculpates absent material misconduct (vs a gross-negligence standard) - and whether covered persons extend to sourcing or placement parties, which LPs will resist.

issues.title: What records must be kept for placement, sourcing, investment decisions, valuation, and conflicts
issues.prompt: What records must be kept for placement, sourcing, investment decisions, valuation, and conflicts?
issues.details: Keep contemporaneous files for solicitation, allocation, valuation, and conflicts decisions - these are the first documents requested in an exam or LP dispute.

issues.category: I. Fund lifecycle and governance (gap review)
issues.title: Side letter and MFN policy
issues.prompt: What side letter policy and MFN (most favored nation) rights will the fund offer?
issues.details: The form permits side letters including better economics. Since placement arrangements and co-invest rights will live in side letters, decide MFN scope early (commonly size-tiered) so one LP's deal doesn't propagate to everyone.

issues.category: I. Fund lifecycle and governance (gap review)
issues.title: Subsequent-closing true-up and interest
issues.prompt: Will subsequent-closing investors pay a true-up plus interest on their share of earlier capital calls and fees?
issues.details: The form permits later closings within a bracketed window. With early warehoused deals, later investors will be buying into appreciated positions - decide the true-up interest rate and whether late LPs share in pre-closing deals at cost.

issues.category: J. Distributions, liability, and tax (gap review)
issues.title: Recycling and reinvestment of proceeds
issues.prompt: May the fund recycle or reinvest disposition proceeds, and within what limits?
issues.details: The memo never addresses recycling, but a secondaries fund with quick flips needs it: without reinvestment rights, early exits shrink deployable capital. Typical formulation permits recycling proceeds received during the investment period up to 100-120% of commitments.

issues.category: J. Distributions, liability, and tax (gap review)
issues.title: In-kind distributions of non-marketable private shares
issues.prompt: Can the fund distribute non-marketable private shares in kind, and how are they valued and transferred given company ROFRs and consent rights?
issues.details: The form only allows non-marketable in-kind distributions at dissolution. For a fund holding private secondaries, end-of-life positions may be unsaleable - the mechanics (valuation, ROFR compliance, LP election to decline) deserve real attention.

issues.category: J. Distributions, liability, and tax (gap review)
issues.title: Tax-exempt and non-U.S. investor accommodations
issues.prompt: Will the fund accept tax-exempt or non-U.S. LPs, and are blockers or feeders needed for UBTI/ECI concerns?
issues.details: Fund-level borrowing (subscription line, bridge) can create UBTI for tax-exempt LPs; non-U.S. LPs care about ECI. Decide whether to offer a blocker/feeder or restrict the investor base, and stay under the 25% ERISA plan-asset threshold.

issues.title: Core Fund Document: Fund LPA / Fund Agreement
issues.prompt: Full governing agreement for the Fund LP.
issues.details: Must authorize secondary strategy, capital calls, expenses, additional vehicles, conflicts, fees, carry, borrowing, and investment limitations.

issues.title: Core Fund Document: Subscription Agreement
issues.prompt: Investor commitments and representations.
issues.details: Accredited investor / qualified purchaser status, bad actor, AML/KYC, tax forms, ERISA reps, side letter integration.

issues.title: Core Fund Document: PPM / Offering Memorandum
issues.prompt: Main disclosure document.
issues.details: Should disclose strategy, secondary risks, placement/sourcing arrangements, conflicts, fees, carry, transfer restrictions, valuation uncertainty.

issues.title: Core Fund Document: Form D and state blue-sky filings
issues.prompt: Securities notice filings after first sale.
issues.details: Coordinate with counsel immediately after first investor becomes contractually committed.

issues.title: Core Fund Document: Side Letter Template
issues.prompt: Special investor terms.
issues.details: MFN, fee/carry discounts, reporting, transfer, co-investment, excuse rights, confidentiality.

issues.title: Sponsor / Management Document: GP LLC Operating Agreement
issues.prompt: Creates legal control of the fund and carry recipient if no CarryCo is used.
issues.details: Must separate voting/control from economics if non-control parties receive carry.

issues.title: Sponsor / Management Document: Manager LLC Operating Agreement
issues.prompt: Ongoing management/advisory business.
issues.details: Defines ownership of management fee profits, authority, expenses, employment/consulting, adviser registration implications.

issues.title: Sponsor / Management Document: Investment Management Agreement
issues.prompt: Fund/GP engages Manager to provide advisory/admin services.
issues.details: Defines delegated authority, fees, reimbursements, termination, indemnity, compliance obligations.

issues.title: Sponsor / Management Document: Sponsor HoldCo Agreement, if used
issues.prompt: Platform-level ownership above Manager and possibly GP entities.
issues.details: Only use if useful for long-term platform economics; avoid giving fund-specific economics accidentally across all funds.

issues.title: Sponsor / Management Document: Fund-specific CarryCo / SponsorCo Agreement, if used
issues.prompt: Splits carry for a particular fund.
issues.details: Useful for Class A voting control, Class B non-voting conditional carry, passive economics, vesting, forfeiture, clawback sharing.

issues.title: Placement / Sourcing Document: Registered Broker-Dealer Placement Agreement
issues.prompt: For registered placement agents.
issues.details: Compensation, tail, permitted investors, offering materials, compliance review, FINRA/SEC obligations, indemnity.

issues.title: Placement / Sourcing Document: Generic Capital Formation / Placement Agreement
issues.prompt: Template for buy-side LP placement relationships.
issues.details: Must be limited by securities-law compliance; specify no authority to bind the fund, no custody, no investment discretion.

issues.title: Placement / Sourcing Document: Conditional Carry Participation Agreement
issues.prompt: Carry or economics based on capital commitments placed.
issues.details: May reference GP/CarryCo operating agreement. Include vesting thresholds, accepted commitments, tail, forfeiture, compliance conditions.

issues.title: Placement / Sourcing Document: Finder / Consultant Agreement
issues.prompt: Only for non-BD relationships if counsel approves.
issues.details: Use carefully. Avoid transaction-based compensation unless counsel confirms path. Include reps and limitations.

issues.title: Placement / Sourcing Document: Sell-Side Sourcing / Origination Agreement
issues.prompt: For deal sources, brokers, consultants, or strategic relationships.
issues.details: Define introduction-only vs negotiation role; compensation; exclusivity; confidentiality; no authority to bind fund.

issues.title: Placement / Sourcing Document: Secondary Transaction / Broker Agreement
issues.prompt: For parties helping acquire or dispose of private securities.
issues.details: Broker-dealer analysis likely needed if compensation is transaction-based or party negotiates securities transactions.

issues.title: Operating Policy / Process: Investment Policy / Whitelist Schedule
issues.prompt: Defines eligible companies and permitted instruments.
issues.details: Include update process for whitelist and approval thresholds.

issues.title: Operating Policy / Process: Allocation Policy
issues.prompt: Allocates opportunities among fund, co-invests, SPVs, parallel funds, affiliates, and future funds.
issues.details: Critical if multiple inbound deal sources and LP-facing channels exist.

issues.title: Operating Policy / Process: Capital Call Policy
issues.prompt: Normal and expedited capital-call procedures.
issues.details: Should handle 3-5 business day calls, emergency calls, reserves, default penalties.

issues.title: Operating Policy / Process: Warehousing Policy
issues.prompt: Permits GP/affiliate to warehouse deals before fund takes them down.
issues.details: Define cost basis, expenses, timing, conflict approvals, disclosure.

issues.title: Operating Policy / Process: Conflict Policy
issues.prompt: Deals with affiliate transactions, sourced deals, side letters, co-invests, valuation, allocation.
issues.details: Coordinate with LPAC provisions.

issues.title: Operating Policy / Process: Valuation Policy
issues.prompt: Valuation of private shares, SPVs, tender interests, forwards, and in-kind distributions.
issues.details: Important due to illiquidity and limited observable prices.

issues.title: Operating Policy / Process: Compliance Policies
issues.prompt: Adviser, broker-dealer interface, AML/KYC, bad actor, privacy, records.
issues.details: Depends on adviser registration/exemption and placement model.
